title: Volatility-Normalized Risk and Exit Improvement

trade_lifecycle_diagnosis.signal_and_entry: Indicators and BUY signals are calculated on the research frame; entries execute at the signal candle close.
trade_lifecycle_diagnosis.position_sizing: Each trade uses 100% of current balance. Risk per trade is therefore not normalized to stop distance.
trade_lifecycle_diagnosis.baseline_exits: The baseline searches fixed percentage stops [1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5] and targets [2.0, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0], then uses one selected pair for validation.
trade_lifecycle_diagnosis.intrabar_execution: Exits start on the candle after entry. If stop and target are both touched, the stop is applied first, which is conservative and unchanged.
trade_lifecycle_diagnosis.cost_model: The generated-candidate research path uses zero fees and has no slippage model. This optimistic limitation is unchanged in order to preserve the Phase 21.1 comparison.
trade_lifecycle_diagnosis.logging_and_validation: Calendar entry/exit timestamps and real trade returns feed the progressive funnel, historical prior-candle regimes, robustness, walk-forward, Monte Carlo, and final readiness.
trade_lifecycle_diagnosis.dominant_phase_21_1_evidence: Both promising candidates had adequate trade samples but profit factors of 1.09-1.10 and drawdowns of 29.61%-32.00%; losses were regime-dependent. Fixed exits across changing volatility were therefore a justified single-factor hypothesis, while 100% sizing remained a known drawdown amplifier.
trade_lifecycle_diagnosis.post_experiment_conclusion: A single universal ATR 1.5x stop / 3.0x target was not a broad improvement. It increased trading frequency and worsened aggregate profit factor, ROI, expectancy, and drawdown. The remaining bottleneck is signal/regime selectivity combined with non-risk-normalized sizing, not insufficiently weak validation.

implementation.timing: ATR is read from the previous completed candle at entry; invalid or missing ATR cannot produce a trade.
implementation.candidate_policy: The original 30 generated candidates remain unchanged and precede 30 deterministic ATR variants with unique IDs. No multiplier was selected from final validation results.
implementation.validation_policy: All gates and thresholds are unchanged. The robustness task budget is derived from configured candidate/window/neighbor counts so every eligible one-year survivor is evaluated.
implementation.report_contract: Full candidate metrics are handed from final selection to the outer benchmark so root and run-local reports reconcile without treating hidden fields as failures.

fixed_vs_atr_generated_candidates.interpretation: A few local pairs improved, but all three profit-factor-plus-drawdown improvements remained unprofitable. Aggregate and robustness evidence does not support promotion of this ATR specification.

monte_carlo_and_final.atr_candidates: Not run: all ATR survivors were rejected by robustness before Monte Carlo, as required by the funnel.
monte_carlo_and_final.fixed_promising_candidates: The Phase 21.1 volume and breakout candidates retained their prior Monte Carlo results and PROMISING_REVIEW status.

remaining_risks: The backtest still allocates 100% of balance per trade and does not normalize position size to stop distance.
Fees and slippage remain zero in generated-candidate research, so reported outcomes are optimistic.
A single predeclared ATR specification cannot establish that all volatility-aware exits are ineffective; expanding multipliers against final-period results would, however, create overfitting risk.
ATR exits more than doubled mean trade count, exposing weak signal selectivity and repeated losses.
The three ATR one-year survivors were concentrated in BNBUSDT and did not show multi-regime consistency.
recommended_next_research_question: Before adding more exit variants, test whether risk-per-trade position sizing or regime-aware entry suppression addresses the drawdown and overtrading bottlenecks using a separately predeclared experiment.
